calendar.matters:
CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Dec-14-2017 Off Calendar

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Service Or, Alternatively Motion To Stay
Off Calendar Nov-03-2017 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Jeff G. Harmeyer, Esq. of Harmeyer Law Group, Tel. 619.231.9800, for defendant and moving party;
No other appearance.

RULING: There being no objection, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on DEFENDANT HILTON WORLDWIDE HOLDINGS INC.'s Motion To Quash Service Or, Alternatively Motion To Stay as follows:
Defendant Hilton Worldwide Holdings, Inc.'s motion to quash service of summons is granted and plaintiffs Yola Inc. and Trevor Harries-Jones' requests for jurisdictional discovery and leave to amend complaint are denied. Plaintiffs have not shown that Hilton, a Delaware corporation with a principal place of business in Virginia, is the "exceptional case" where it has such "continuous and systematic" affiliations with California that it is subject to the general jurisdiction in this state per Daimler AG v. Bauman (2014) 134 S.Ct. 746, 760. The fact that Hilton has extensive business activities in California is not sufficient to render it subject to the general jurisdiction where it also has extensive business activities in at least 37 other states. A "corporation that operates in many places can scarcely be deemed at home in all of them." (BNSF Ry. Co. v. Tyrrell (2017) 137 S.Ct. 1549, 1559). Plaintiffs have also failed to show "a connection between the forum and the specific claims at issue" to support specific jurisdiction over Hilton. (Bristol-Myers Squibb Co. v. Superior Court of California, San Francisco County (2017) 137 S.Ct. 1773, 1781). Plaintiffs' causes of action for premises liability, negligence and loss of prospective economic advantage are all based on the injury sustained by Mr. Harries-Jones in Louisiana. The conduct giving rise to his claims, the alleged defendant's negligence, occurred in Louisiana. Plaintiffs' claims are not substantially connected to the Hilton's advertising in California. This case is unlike Snowney v. Harrah's Entertainment, Inc. (2005) 35 Cal.4th 1054 where the plaintiff's causes of action for unfair competition, breach of contract, unjust enrichment and false advertising had a substantial connection with defendant company's advertising to California residents of their Nevada hotel. Plaintiffs have not explained how an amendment to the complaint will cure its jurisdictional deficiencies. Plaintiffs have not shown how their proposed jurisdictional discovery has a reasonable prospect to yield any evidentiary support for a ruling in their favor in light of Daimler and BNSF.

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: G. Gonzales; Court Reporter: Reyes F. Hunter, CSR #6576, email:rhunter@sftc.org.
